votes.reason: The Python code correctly implements the Coq logic, using zip to match the behavior of count_diff and correctly slicing the array to match firstn and skipn.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq logic, including the list slicing for the first and second halves, and the element-wise comparison using zip which matches the behavior of the Coq count_diff function.

The Python code correctly translates the Coq logic, accurately slicing the array into halves, reversing the second half, and counting the differences using zip, which mirrors the behavior of the Coq count_diff function.

The Python code correctly implements the same logic as the Coq code: count_diff iterates through pairs of elements and counts mismatches; smallest_change_impl splits the array into first half (first half_len elements) and second half (last half_len elements), reverses the second half, and counts differences. The slicing arr[:half_len] matches firstn, arr[length - half_len:] matches skipn (len - half_len), and the counting logic is identical. Both pre and spec functions match their Coq counterparts.

The Python code correctly implements the same logic as the Coq code. count_diff iterates through pairs from l1 and l2, counting mismatches. smallest_change_impl computes half_len as integer division of length by 2, takes the first half_len elements, takes the last half_len elements (via skipn(len-half_len) in Coq, which equals arr[length-half_len:] in Python), reverses the second half, and counts differences. The pre and spec functions also match: pre always returns True, and spec checks if n equals the implementation result.

The Python code correctly implements the same logic as the Coq code. count_diff iterates through pairs of elements from two lists, counting mismatches. smallest_change_impl computes half_len as integer division of length by 2, takes the first half_len elements, takes the last half_len elements (via skipn (len - half_len) in Coq, equivalent to arr[length - half_len:] in Python), reverses the second half, and counts differences. The pre and spec functions also match: pre always returns True, and spec checks if n equals the computed result.

Python mirrors the Coq logic: it takes the first and last half (using floor division), reverses the last half, and counts pairwise mismatches with zip, matching count_diff and smallest_change_impl behavior on all inputs.

Both versions split the list into halves (ignoring the middle element if odd), reverse the second half, and count mismatches pairwise. Slicing, reversing, and counting logic match Coq’s definitions for all inputs.

Both implementations compare the first half of the list with the reverse of the second half (excluding the middle element for odd lengths), counting mismatches. Python slicing and reversed mirror Coq’s firstn/skipn/rev, and the pairwise count via zip matches the recursive count. Pre/spec are identical.
